A gentle intro to Speculative Decoding (how LLMs generate tokens quickly)
In this article we will discuss how LLM decoding works, the main bottlenecks for speed, and the way researchers have been pushing the boundaries to make it even better.
In this article, we will talk about why autoregressive decoding is slow. How we have been using Speculative Decoding (SD) to make it wayyy faster. And how a new technique has been published this week that makes SD even faster with SSD - Speculative Speculative Decoding!
Tanishq Kumar
@tanishqkumar07

I've been working on a new LLM inference algorithm. It's called Speculative Speculative Decoding (SSD) and it's up to 2x faster than the strongest inference engines in the world. Collab w/

If you clicked on this article, you probably already know the answer to the next question. For the sake of completion, I will still include it in this article anyway.

So how do LLMs generate tokens

LLMs generate tokens one token at a time. At each timestep, the model looks at all the past tokens in it's current sequence, and outputs a probability distribution over it's entire vocabulary.
So if a model has 10,000 tokens in its vocabulary (very low number compared to today's LLM standards) the model is generating a probability distribution for each token at each step of generation. Tens of billions of parameters get activated within the model to generate these 10,000 floating point numbers!
For each token, the LLM's computational complexity is O(n d^2) where n = number of tokens in the prefix, and d = dimensions.
For an entire sequence, the LLM's computational complexity is O(n^2 d^2)

The GPU Bottleneck

LLMs have a sequential dependency. You literally cannot generate token t_100​ until you've finished generating token t_1 to t_99. The model needs to see "t1,t2,...,t99​" before it can predict what comes next. This is just the nature of autoregressive generation - each token is conditioned on all previous tokens.
This is the single biggest bottleneck in LLM inference.
But what if we could guess what the next few tokens might be, and then verify them all at once in parallel? That's where Speculative Decoding comes in.
Before I explain how Speculative Decoding works, let's first consider a couple of really interesting things that LLMs do. These ideas are essentially what will help us "discover" the aha moment of speculative decoding.
Observation 1: Generating tokens is autoregressive, but reading past tokens are parallel
  • The autoregressive-ness is only when generating new output tokens
  • When processing input tokens, LLMs are able to parallel-process the entire sequence!
Observation 2: Many Tokens Are Easy to Predict
  • Not all tokens require a LLM to predict. Infact most tokens are what we call "low entropy" - i.e. they are very predictable!
  • For example, if the input is "The paper that introduced Transformers is", the next few tokens are incredibly obvious!
Observation 3: Memory Bandwidth, Not Compute, Is the Bottleneck
  • Each forward pass requires loading billions of parameters from GPU memory (VRAM → compute cores). For large models, this memory transfer takes longer than the actual matrix multiplications.
  • It will be nice if we can do more with each forward pass (parallel process) than just produce one token.

Speculative Decoding: The Idea

Speculative Decoding (SD) solves this bottleneck with a simple two-step process: guess multiple tokens with a smaller model, then verify them all in parallel with the larger model.

Here's how it works:

Step 1: Speculation

Instead of using your expensive target model to generate tokens one by one, you use a small, fast draft model to predict the next K tokens (typically K=4 to 8).
This draft model is much smaller - like 500M params instead of 70B. It's 10-20× faster, but less accurate. That's fine, because we're just guessing.

Step 2: Verification

Now here's the key: the target model verifies all K drafted tokens in a single parallel forward pass. Instead of doing 4 sequential passes, you do 1 parallel pass over all 4 tokens at once.
The target model computes the probability for each drafted token​, then decides which ones to accept and which to reject!
The Acceptance Rule Each token gets accepted with probability:
  • If the big LLM (or target LLM) likes token T more than the draft model -> Just accept it
  • If the big LLM likes token T less than draft model -> only accept by the ratio.
Example:
Prefix: "The capital of France is ____"
Draft model says: "The next token is 'Paris' with 60% confidence"
Case 1: Target model says "Paris" with 80% confidence
  • Always accept — target agrees or is even more confident
Case 2: Target model says "Paris" with 30% confidence
  • Ratio: 0.3/0.6=0.5
  • Accept with 50% probability — target is less sure, so we're cautious

Step 3: Bonus Token

Tokens are accepted sequentially until the first rejection. When you reject at position k, you throw away everything after that and sample a "bonus token".
Basically, tokens are checked left to right. As soon as one gets rejected, you throw away everything after it.
Example:
Draft: ["The", "cat", "jumped", "over"]
Target checks:
  1. "The" → Accept ✓
  2. "cat" → Accept ✓
  3. "jumped" → Reject ✗
  4. "over" → Never checked, thrown away
You keep ["The", "cat"] and discard the rest.


Step 4: Residual Distribution

When a token is rejected, the bonus token is not sampled from the target LLM's own probability.
This is important!
Instead the bonus token is sampled from what's called the residual distribution. The residual distribution is the Target LLM probability of a token MINUS the Draft LLM probability of that token.
So if "jumped" was rejected, the target samples a replacement - let's say it samples "ran" instead.
Current sequence: ["The", "cat", "ran"]. The draft model generates K more tokens with this new prefix!

The Loop

Draft → Verify → (Accept or Reject + Correct) → Draft → Verify → ... This loop continues until:
  • You hit the max token length, OR
  • An [EOS] (end-of-sequence) token is generated


Why does Speculative Decoding work?

The speedup comes from two things:
  1. Parallel verification: Instead of K (=4) sequential target model calls, you do 1 parallel call
  2. Cheap speculation: The draft model is fast enough that 4 draft calls cost less than 1 target call!
If your draft model accepts 3 out of 4 tokens on average, you're generating ~3 tokens per target model call instead of 1. That's a 2-3× speedup right there.

The Bottleneck with Speculative Decoding

Speculative Decoding has its own bottleneck Here's the problem: drafting and verification are still sequential. You draft, then you wait. Verify, then you wait. Draft again, then wait again.
Draft → Wait → Verify → Draft → Wait → Verify
The draft model sits idle during verification. The target model sits idle during drafting. We've parallelized token generation, but we haven't parallelized the draft/verify cycle itself.
That's the bottleneck that Speculative Speculative Decoding fixes.


The New Kid on the Block: Speculative Speculative Decoding (SSD)

We finally arrive at this week's cool paper: Speculative Speculative Decoding (SSD). No not a typo.
The idea is simple:
Let's run the Drafting and Verification in parallel
SSD Algorithm works in the three steps mentioned below:

Step 1: Initial Draft (Same as SD)

Draft model generates K tokens: ["jumped", "over", "the", "fence"]

Step 2: Parallel Verification + Preemptive Speculation

While the target model is verifying those 4 tokens, the draft model:
  1. Predicts likely verification outcomes: "What will the target accept/reject?" Maybe: Accept all 4 tokens ✓✓✓✓ Maybe: Accept first 2, reject at position 3 ✓✓✗✗ Maybe: Reject immediately at position 1 ✗✗✗✗
  2. Pre-drafts speculations for each predicted outcome 0: If all accepted: Draft next 4 tokens: ["jumped", "over", "the", "fence" ,"and", "ran", "away", "fast"] If rejected at position 3: Draft from that point: ["jumped", "over", "a", "tall", "wall"] If rejected at position 1: Draft from start: ["ran", "across", "quickly"]
All of this happens simultaneously while the target is still processing!
We store all these additional generations inside a cache. The cache key is a list of prefix tokens, and the value is the drafts after it.

Step 3: Instant Return When Verification Completes

When the target finishes verification, we check:
  • Did the actual outcome match one of our predictions? YES → Return the pre-prepared speculation immediately (zero drafting overhead!) NO → Fall back to regular drafting from the actual position

Saguaro

While the SSD framework is elegant in theory, making it work in practice requires solving three critical challenges.
  • How much to pre-decode?
  • How to increase cache-hits?
The Saguaro algorithm addresses them!
Q. What is Saguaro?
Saguaro is the name of the optimized algorithm that implements Speculative Speculative Decoding (SSD)
The name comes from a cactus which has branching heads. Just like SSD has branching generating paths.

1. Fan-Out Strategy

So obviously we can't generate ALL possible verification outcomes - that will be crazy..
Saguaro's cast this as a constrained optimization problem. Given a budget, Saguaro uses a "fan-out strategy"
Fan-out refers to how many branches or alternatives you explore at each decision point. In Saguaro's case, it's how many possible next tokens you prepare speculations for at each position in the sequence.

The draft model has limited time during verification. Let's say verification takes time T_v​ and the draft model can generate tokens at rate T_d​ per token.
This means we know a budget B = [T_v / T_d]. This is the maximum number of speculative tokens the draft model can prepare while verification is running.
This budget B is crucial: if we exceed it, the draft model won't finish before verification completes, and we've wasted computation preparing speculations we can't use.
Let's say you have a budget B=7 speculative tokens. Saguaro might decide:
Position 0 (prefix) → Prepare for top 3 tokens: ["the", "a", "an"]
  • This uses 3 slots from our budget
Position 1 (if we continue) → For each of those 3, prepare top 2 continuations
  • This would need 3 × 2 = 6 more slots...
  • But we are overflowing our budget coz 3 + 6 = 9 > 7
Smart allocation: Following optimization, we use F₀ = 2 (2 tokens at pos 0) and F₁ = 2 (2 continuations each). This gives us 6 tokens, which meets our budget!
The fan-out values determine the shape of your speculation tree—wider at early positions (more alternatives) or deeper (longer sequences for fewer alternatives).
We balances breadth and depth to maximize the chance the actual verification outcome is already in our cache

2. Saguaro Sampling: How Do We Increase Cache Hits?

Standard sampling makes bonus tokens unpredictable—they could be anything in the vocabulary, making cache hits rare.
Saguaro's solution: Introduce Saguaro Sampling, a sampling scheme that interpolates between high cache hit rate and high speculative acceptance rate.
In standard speculative decoding, when the target model rejects a draft token, it samples a bonus token from the residual distribution.
Remember bonus token = when the main LLM rejects the draft model's tokens, it also proposes the token it prefers instead. (explained in the vanilla SD section)
In standard speculative decoding, here's what happens when the target model rejects a draft token:
  1. The target model says "Nope, that draft token is wrong"
  2. The target model samples a corrected token (called the "bonus token")
  3. This bonus token could be anything in the vocabulary (50,000+ possibilities!)
For SSD: We want to predict what this bonus token will be before the rejection happens, so we can have a speculation ready. But how do you predict something that could be anything?
Answer: You can't... unless you cheat a little and make the bonus token more predictable.
Remember that the target LLM samples from the residual distribution? That is P_target(t) - P_draft(t) where t is the token.
The residual distribution depends on the draft token distribution as well. So why not manipulate the draft distribution to make cache hits more probable?
It works by increasing the residual probability mass on the top draft tokens, effectively biasing the bonus token to land within the pre-cached speculations.
Step 1: Pick Your Favorite Tokens (from the DRAFT model)
When building the Saguaro cache, you:
  1. Look at the draft model's predictions (its logits/probabilities)
  2. Pick the top-F most likely tokens according to the draft model
  3. These F tokens become your "cache keys" — the ones you'll prepare speculations for

Step 2: Deliberately Lower Those Draft Probabilities
Now here's the trick: When the draft model actually generates speculations (samples tokens for the next round), you modify its sampling process:
  • Take those same top-F draft tokens you identified
  • Multiply their probabilities by C<1 (downweight them)
  • Re-normalize and sample
Example:
Draft model originally predicts:
  • "cat": 0.5 (top-1, so we cache it)
  • "dog": 0.3 (top-2, so we cache it)
  • "bird": 0.2
During Saguaro sampling with C=0.2C=0.2:
  • "cat": 0.5 × 0.2 = 0.1 ← downweighted
  • "dog": 0.3 × 0.2 = 0.06 ← downweighted
  • "bird": 0.2 (unchanged)
  • Re-normalize: (0.1 + 0.06 + 0.2) = 0.36 → "cat" becomes 0.1/0.36 ≈ 0.28, etc.
Step 3: Verification (target model runs)
  1. Target model verifies the speculation
  2. If rejection occurs → bonus token sampled from residual
  3. Because you downweighted the draft probabilities, the residual now has higher probability on your cached tokens!
  4. High chance of cache hit!
Remember the fundamental principle of speculative decoding:
The draft model is just making proposals. The target model has the final say.
No matter how you bias the draft model, the acceptance rule ensures the final output is distributed as p_target. In vanilla SD, we do this by using the residual distribution trick.
There's a lot of cool math that goes into these papers so we never disrespect this one principle. I encourage yall to read through the main papers on Arxiv or on Paper Breakdown.
SSD Paper on Arxiv:
arxiv.org/abs/2603.0325
SSD Paper on Paper Breakdown for context-aware LLM chat:
paperbreakdown.com/abs/2603.0325
A survey paper on SD that you will surely enjoy:
https://arxiv.org/abs/2401.07851
